title: Browser smoke full visible prose capture (domain hardening checkpoint)

cases.prompt: I found a wallet with cash and an ID in a cafe, and I am tempted to keep the cash because my rent is due.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Taking the cash would be theft, regardless of your financial pressure—return the wallet intact.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Your rent crisis doesn't transform theft into something else—the wallet has an owner whose day you'd ruin.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I found a wallet with cash and an ID in a cafe, and I am tempted to keep the cash because my rent is due.
Core reading: Your rent crisis doesn't transform theft into something else—the wallet has an owner whose day you'd ruin.
Gita analysis: The Gita warns against letting rajas (passion/urgency) override dharma. Your financial pressure is real, but using it to justify taking another's property reflects the very attachment-driven action that perpetuates suffering. Right action remains right action regardless of personal circumstances.

cards.title: Gita Verse
cards.primary_text: You become either your own ally or your own saboteur through action. It speaks directly to the tension around action, restraint. It also fits details like found-property in your situation. Its emphasis aligns with the main pressure in this dilemma.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: This verse says your next action either strengthens you or weakens you. Choosing the clean action under pressure protects the kind of person you become.

sections.label: Show Gita anchor
sections.text:
Verse: 6.5
English: Let one lift oneself by oneself; let one not degrade oneself. For the self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
Hindi: अपने द्वारा अपने आप का उद्धार करे, अपने को गिराए नहीं। क्योंकि आत्मा ही आत्मा का मित्र है, और आत्मा ही आत्मा का शत्रु है।

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: You buy days of relief by polishing your inner narration and dodging the one detail you do not want said aloud. (Normalizing taking shortcuts when stressed, eroding your ethical… gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: You get fluent at plausible deniability—not as a villain move, but as a fatigue move.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: You buy days of relief by polishing your inner narration and dodging the one detail you do not want said aloud. (Normalizing taking shortcuts when stressed, eroding your ethical… gets louder when nothing is named.)

sections.label: Long-term - Explain simply
sections.text: You get fluent at plausible deniability—not as a villain move, but as a fatigue move.

sections.label: Why this applies here

Core reading: Your rent crisis doesn't transform theft into something else—the wallet has an owner whose day you'd ruin.
Gita analysis: The Gita warns against letting rajas (passion/urgency) override dharma. Your financial pressure is real, but using it to justify taking another's property reflects the very attachment-driven action that perpetuates suffering. Right action remains right action regardless of personal circumstances.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by immediate relief, one guided by ownership and restraint.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: I need relief now, so I can treat this wallet as an exception.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Keep the cash while telling yourself returning the ID is enough.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: The compromise hides inside a partial good action.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: My need is real, but the money still belongs to someone else.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Return the wallet intact and seek rent help without making another person pay for it.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You address pressure without training yourself to call theft necessity.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Name Financial desperation creating moral blindness to obvious theft without adjectives for sixty seconds on paper; delete the story lines; pick the smallest external step that still matches what is left.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Do the clean external action first: return what is not yours. Then solve your pressure without making the owner pay for it.

sections.label: Why this applies here
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I found a wallet with cash and an ID in a cafe, and I am tempted to keep the cash because my rent is due.
Core reading: Your rent crisis doesn't transform theft into something else—the wallet has an owner whose day you'd ruin.

Your financial pressure is real, but using it to justify taking another's property reflects the very attachment-driven action that perpetuates suffering. Right action remains right action regardless of personal circumstances.

sections.text:

cards.title: Share Layer
cards.primary_text: Urgency can be real without turning a stranger's wallet into permission.

sections.label: Reflective question
sections.text: Can you solve this pressure without borrowing from someone else's harm?

cases.prompt: My manager publicly took credit for my work, and I am considering correcting the record in the same public meeting.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Correcting the record publicly serves truth but requires skillful timing and tone to avoid workplace disruption.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Your manager's action violates professional norms and your legitimate claim to recognition. The urge to correct immediately is understandable but choosing the right forum matters more than speed.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My manager publicly took credit for my work, and I am considering correcting the record in the same public meeting.
Core reading: Your manager's action violates professional norms and your legitimate claim to recognition. The urge to correct immediately is understandable but choosing the right forum matters more than speed.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that while we have the right to our actions, we don't control their fruits. Here, you control whether and how to correct the record, but not how others will receive it. Acting from wounded ego rather than duty clouds judgment.

cards.title: Closest Gita Lens
cards.primary_text: Closest lens: This is concept-level guidance, not a scripture quote. The pull here is mixed, not one-dimensional. The framing is useful, while the modern details still need case-level judgment. Use this as a lens, not a command.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The Gita teaches that while we have the right to our actions, we don't control their fruits. Here, you control whether and how to correct the record, but not how others will receive it. Acting from wounded ego rather than duty clouds judgment.

sections.label: Why this stays provisional
sections.text: This is not a direct verse verdict. The situation needs judgment beyond a single quote.

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: You trade focus for vigilance: small wins in meetings, larger tabs kept open on who said what. (Ego-driven response could escalate into ongoing workplace conflict gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: What people remember is how pressure felt in the room; the factual record stops being the headline.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: You trade focus for vigilance: small wins in meetings, larger tabs kept open on who said what. (Ego-driven response could escalate into ongoing workplace conflict gets louder when nothing is named.)

Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that while we have the right to our actions, we don't control their fruits. Here, you control whether and how to correct the record, but not how others will receive it. Acting from wounded ego rather than duty clouds judgment.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by status recovery, one guided by factual correction and proportion.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: I need to reclaim face now, even if the room turns into combat.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Correct them publicly to win the room back.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: The fact gets blurred by the spectacle of the correction.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: The record matters, and so does the channel that keeps the correction usable.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Document the facts, ask privately for correction, and escalate only if the pattern continues.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You protect truth while reducing avoidable collateral conflict.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Put the dispute in one email thread with specifics and dates; ask for a correction window; if none, attach the same packet to whoever owns integrity reviews—keep the audience shrinking, not growing.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Protect the truth with evidence and process before using public confrontation.

sections.label: Why this applies here

cases.prompt: I can legally open an alcohol shop in my neighborhood, but I worry it may increase harm even though it would support my family.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Legal business rights don't override the weight of enabling addiction and community harm for personal gain.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: You recognize the tension between legal permission and moral responsibility - this awareness itself points toward finding alternative paths that support family without compromising community wellbeing.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I can legally open an alcohol shop in my neighborhood, but I worry it may increase harm even though it would support my family.
Core reading: You recognize the tension between legal permission and moral responsibility - this awareness itself points toward finding alternative paths that support family without compromising community wellbeing.
Gita analysis: The Gita distinguishes between what we can do and what upholds dharma. Your livelihood affects not just personal karma but creates ripples through every family touched by addiction. Right livelihood means earning through means that don't systematically harm others.

cards.title: Gita Verse
cards.primary_text: Work aligned with your nature is preferable to borrowed ideals. It speaks directly to the tension around duty, right-livelihood. It also fits details like career-crossroads, livelihood-harm-tradeoff in your situation. Its emphasis aligns with the main pressure in this dilemma.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: This verse is not saying every legal job is clean. It asks whether the work itself fits a responsible way of living.

sections.label: Show Gita anchor
sections.text:
Verse: 18.47
English: Better one's own dharma imperfect than another's well performed. Performing work suited to one's nature, one incurs no sin.
Hindi: अपना धर्म गुणरहित भी दूसरे के अच्छी तरह आचरण किए हुए धर्म से श्रेष्ठ है। स्वभाव से नियत कर्म करता हुआ मनुष्य पाप को प्राप्त नहीं होता।

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: Relief if you vent sideways: someone else carries the stress as gossip instead of as information. (Normalizing harm as acceptable cost of doing business gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: Trust becomes positional: who is 'in' on edits to the story, and which facts stop traveling with the kids.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: Relief if you vent sideways: someone else carries the stress as gossip instead of as information. (Normalizing harm as acceptable cost of doing business gets louder when nothing is named.)

sections.label: Long-term - Explain simply
sections.text: Trust becomes positional: who is 'in' on edits to the story, and which facts stop traveling with the kids.

Gita analysis: The Gita distinguishes between what we can do and what upholds dharma. Your livelihood affects not just personal karma but creates ripples through every family touched by addiction. Right livelihood means earning through means that don't systematically harm others.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by short-term revenue, one guided by livelihood with social accountability.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: If the business is legal, that should end the ethical question.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Treat legality and family need as enough reason to ignore predictable harm.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: Legality can hide a cost that still lands on neighbors.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Family support matters, and the harm footprint still has to count.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Look for income that supports your family without depending on your neighborhood's weakness.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You keep provider duty without externalizing the ethical bill.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Write a short paragraph you would stand by if forwarded—then send it once, to the smallest circle that can act on it; let silence after that be a choice, not a dodge.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Do not treat family need and legality as the whole answer. Look for income that does not depend on predictable harm.

cases.prompt: I have developed desire for my close friend's partner, and they seem interested too, but acting on it would betray my friend.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: This attraction tests whether you value momentary chemistry over established trust and friendship.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The pull toward forbidden connection often signals unmet needs elsewhere - for excitement, validation, or escape from routine. The mutual interest creates an intoxicating bubble that feels more significant than it is.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I have developed desire for my close friend's partner, and they seem interested too, but acting on it would betray my friend.
Core reading: The pull toward forbidden connection often signals unmet needs elsewhere - for excitement, validation, or escape from routine. The mutual interest creates an intoxicating bubble that feels more significant than it is.
Gita analysis: Krishna warns against actions born from rajas (passion) that appear pleasurable initially but lead to suffering. The mind clouded by desire loses sight of dharma. True strength lies not in conquest but in mastery over one's impulses when they conflict with righteous conduct.

cards.title: Closest Gita Lens
cards.primary_text: Closest lens: The Gita still offers useful lenses through duty, intention, non-harm, restraint, and welfare-of-all, but this modern structure does not map cleanly to one verse without forced certainty. Use this as a lens, not a command.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Krishna warns against actions born from rajas (passion) that appear pleasurable initially but lead to suffering. The mind clouded by desire loses sight of dharma. True strength lies not in conquest but in mastery over one's impulses when they conflict with righteous conduct.

sections.label: Why this stays provisional
sections.text: This is not a direct verse verdict. The situation needs judgment beyond a single quote.

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: Short term you win quiet at the dinner table; the hardest sentence does not get spoken where it belongs. (Using chemistry to avoid examining what's missing in your current life gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: Trust becomes positional: who is 'in' on edits to the story, and which facts stop traveling with the kids.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: Short term you win quiet at the dinner table; the hardest sentence does not get spoken where it belongs. (Using chemistry to avoid examining what's missing in your current life gets louder when nothing is named.)

Gita analysis: Krishna warns against actions born from rajas (passion) that appear pleasurable initially but lead to suffering. The mind clouded by desire loses sight of dharma. True strength lies not in conquest but in mastery over one's impulses when they conflict with righteous conduct.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by attraction and secrecy, one guided by trust and boundary.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: This attraction is special enough to suspend ordinary loyalty.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Follow the attraction and rename betrayal as rare chemistry.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: The story flatters desire while trust carries the damage later.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Strong feeling does not erase responsibility to people already trusting me.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Create distance, refuse secrecy, and protect the friendship before desire writes the story.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You avoid a short thrill that hardens into a long trust debt.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Tell the one person who truly needs the fact first, in one sitting, with no audience; refuse to triangulate through kids or group chats; schedule a second conversation instead of a second performance.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Create distance before secrecy turns attraction into betrayal.

cases.prompt: My aging parent is refusing hospitalization after a serious diagnosis, and I am torn between respecting them and forcing treatment.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Respecting your parent's autonomy while ensuring they understand consequences honors both their dignity and your duty of care.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: A competent adult's refusal of treatment reflects their sovereignty over their own life journey, even when that journey approaches its end.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My aging parent is refusing hospitalization after a serious diagnosis, and I am torn between respecting them and forcing treatment.
Core reading: A competent adult's refusal of treatment reflects their sovereignty over their own life journey, even when that journey approaches its end.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that each soul has its own path and timing. Just as Krishna respects Arjuna's agency even in crisis, you must honor your parent's choice while fulfilling your duty to ensure they're making an informed decision.

cards.title: Closest Gita Lens
cards.primary_text: Closest lens: This is concept-level guidance, not a scripture quote. The framing is useful, while the modern details still need case-level judgment. Use this as a lens, not a command.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The Gita teaches that each soul has its own path and timing. Just as Krishna respects Arjuna's agency even in crisis, you must honor your parent's choice while fulfilling your duty to ensure they're making an informed decision.

sections.label: Why this stays provisional
sections.text: This is not a direct verse verdict. The situation needs judgment beyond a single quote.

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: You buy a few calm days by tightening the family narrative—then the next surprise lands louder. (Conflating your anxiety about loss with their best interests gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: People learn what not to ask you; intimacy shrinks to what can be safely performed.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: You buy a few calm days by tightening the family narrative—then the next surprise lands louder. (Conflating your anxiety about loss with their best interests gets louder when nothing is named.)

sections.label: Long-term - Explain simply
sections.text: People learn what not to ask you; intimacy shrinks to what can be safely performed.

sections.label: Why this applies here
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My aging parent is refusing hospitalization after a serious diagnosis, and I am torn between respecting them and forcing treatment.
Core reading: A competent adult's refusal of treatment reflects their sovereignty over their own life journey, even when that journey approaches its end.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that each soul has its own path and timing. Just as Krishna respects Arjuna's agency even in crisis, you must honor your parent's choice while fulfilling your duty to ensure they're making an informed decision.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by fear-based control, one guided by care with autonomy.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: If I force treatment, I am automatically being the better caregiver.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Override their refusal without first building informed, supported consent.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: Control can look like care while quietly erasing the person's agency.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Urgency is real, and dignity still includes informed choice.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Clarify risks, involve support, and push for consent-based treatment before coercion.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You hold safety and respect together instead of sacrificing one to the other.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Write a short paragraph you would stand by if forwarded—then send it once, to the smallest circle that can act on it; let silence after that be a choice, not a dodge.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Care means making sure they understand, not taking control because you are afraid.

sections.label: Why this applies here
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My aging parent is refusing hospitalization after a serious diagnosis, and I am torn between respecting them and forcing treatment.
Core reading: A competent adult's refusal of treatment reflects their sovereignty over their own life journey, even when that journey approaches its end.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that each soul has its own path and timing. Just as Krishna respects Arjuna's agency even in crisis, you must honor your parent's choice while fulfilling your duty to ensure they're making an informed decision.

cases.prompt: I am considering cosmetic surgery mainly because I feel insecure and want social approval, though the procedure is legal and safe.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Seeking surgical validation for social insecurity typically deepens the very wound it promises to heal.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The desire springs from believing your worth depends on meeting external standards rather than recognizing inherent value.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I am considering cosmetic surgery mainly because I feel insecure and want social approval, though the procedure is legal and safe.
Core reading: The desire springs from believing your worth depends on meeting external standards rather than recognizing inherent value.
Gita analysis: Krishna teaches that identifying with the temporary body rather than the eternal self creates suffering. Seeking peace through bodily changes while ignoring the mind's patterns parallels Arjuna's initial desire to flee battle rather than face internal conflicts.

cases.prompt: My parent was abusive for years, and I am considering going no contact even though relatives say it is my duty to stay connected.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Protecting yourself from ongoing harm takes precedence over maintaining harmful family connections out of duty.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Years of abuse have created a legitimate need for protection. Family duty doesn't require accepting ongoing harm.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My parent was abusive for years, and I am considering going no contact even though relatives say it is my duty to stay connected.
Core reading: Years of abuse have created a legitimate need for protection. Family duty doesn't require accepting ongoing harm.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches righteous action even when it conflicts with conventional duty. Krishna advises Arjuna to fight even against relatives when dharma requires it. Your duty to protect your wellbeing supersedes maintaining harmful connections.

cards.title: Closest Gita Lens
cards.primary_text: Closest lens: This is concept-level guidance, not a scripture quote. Related grounding appears in Chapter 6. The dilemma turns on missing context, so this stays provisional. cases.prompt: I want to marry someone I love, but my family rejects the relationship because of caste and threatens to cut ties.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Choosing authentic love over caste prejudice reflects evolved consciousness, though the family rupture carries real weight.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The tension between inherited social identity and chosen personal bonds reveals how caste thinking fragments human connection.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I want to marry someone I love, but my family rejects the relationship because of caste and threatens to cut ties.
Core reading: The tension between inherited social identity and chosen personal bonds reveals how caste thinking fragments human connection.
Gita analysis: Krishna teaches that true dharma transcends birth-based divisions. Acting from love rather than social fear aligns with seeing the same consciousness in all beings, though the path requires accepting the karma of disrupted relationships.

cards.title: Gita Verse
cards.primary_text: A wise person sees equal dignity across social categories. It speaks directly to the tension around compassion, equality. It also fits details like caste-or-identity-boundary, family-disapproval in your situation. Its emphasis aligns with the main pressure in this dilemma.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: This verse cuts through status labels. It supports seeing a person's dignity beyond caste identity.

sections.label: Show Gita anchor
sections.text:
Verse: 5.18
English: The wise see with equal vision a learned brahmin, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and an outcast.
Hindi: विद्या और विनय से युक्त ब्राह्मण में, गाय में, हाथी में, कुत्ते में, और चाण्डाल में भी — पंडित लोग समान दृष्टि रखते हैं।

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:

Long-term: People learn what not to ask you; intimacy shrinks to what can be safely performed.

cases.prompt: A restaurant treated me badly, and I want to post an anonymous scathing review that might damage their business reputation.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Anonymous retaliation through reviews weaponizes truth-telling against its own purpose of constructive communication.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The desire for anonymous retaliation reveals how hurt transforms into harm when we bypass direct resolution.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: A restaurant treated me badly, and I want to post an anonymous scathing review that might damage their business reputation.
Core reading: The desire for anonymous retaliation reveals how hurt transforms into harm when we bypass direct resolution.
Gita analysis: The Gita warns against action born from anger and attachment to results. Your wish to damage their reputation shows deep investment in outcomes rather than right action. True strength lies in addressing grievances directly or releasing them entirely.

cases.prompt: As a doctor, I know a patient has a terminal diagnosis, and the family asks me to hide it from the patient to preserve hope.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Withholding terminal diagnosis from a competent patient violates their fundamental right to truth and self-determination.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The family's request stems from love but denies the patient's capacity for courage and choice. Truth enables dignity in facing mortality.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: As a doctor, I know a patient has a terminal diagnosis, and the family asks me to hide it from the patient to preserve hope.
Core reading: The family's request stems from love but denies the patient's capacity for courage and choice. Truth enables dignity in facing mortality.
Gita analysis: Krishna emphasizes that the soul is eternal and the body temporary. Hiding death's approach prevents the patient from preparing spiritually and practically. True compassion enables conscious transition, not enforced ignorance.

cards.title: Gita Verse
cards.primary_text: Divine qualities hold truth and compassion together. It speaks directly to the tension around compassion, nonharm. It also fits details like truth-compassion-conflict in your situation. Its emphasis aligns with the main pressure in this dilemma.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: This verse joins truth with compassion. The point is not to be blunt, but not to hide what the patient has a right to know.

sections.label: Show Gita anchor
sections.text:
Verse: 16.1-3
English: Non-violence, truth, absence of anger, renunciation, peace, abstention from slander, compassion to all beings, non-covetousness, gentleness, modesty, absence of fickleness — these are the qualities of one born to divine estate.
Hindi: अहिंसा, सत्य, अक्रोध, त्याग, शांति, चुगली न करना, प्राणियों पर दया, लोभ का अभाव, मृदुता, लज्जा, चपलता का अभाव — ये दैवी संपदा के लक्षण हैं।

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: You buy a few calm days by tightening the family narrative—then the next surprise lands louder. (Creating precedent where families control patient information access gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: The workaround becomes habit—honesty deferred until 'after the holidays' becomes its own kind of policy.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: You buy a few calm days by tightening the family narrative—then the next surprise lands louder. (Creating precedent where families control patient information access gets louder when nothing is named.)

sections.label: Long-term - Explain simply
sections.text: The workaround becomes habit—honesty deferred until 'after the holidays' becomes its own kind of policy.

sections.label: Why this applies here
sections.text:
Dilemma context: As a doctor, I know a patient has a terminal diagnosis, and the family asks me to hide it from the patient to preserve hope.
Core reading: The family's request stems from love but denies the patient's capacity for courage and choice. Truth enables dignity in facing mortality.
Gita analysis: Krishna emphasizes that the soul is eternal and the body temporary. Hiding death's approach prevents the patient from preparing spiritually and practically. True compassion enables conscious transition, not enforced ignorance.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by fear-shielding, one guided by compassionate disclosure and consent.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Withholding truth is kinder if the family feels less afraid.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Hide the diagnosis because the family's fear feels kinder in the moment.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: Temporary comfort can quietly remove the patient's right to choose.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Care includes truth-sharing with timing, support, and dignity.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Disclose with compassion, pacing, and support while respecting the patient's right to know.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You preserve agency without abandoning emotional care.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Name the trade in one sentence your future self would sign; pick the channel that protects dignity more than drama; end with a clear next date, not a moral flourish.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Tell the truth with support, timing, and dignity rather than hiding it for comfort.

sections.label: Why this applies here
sections.text:
Dilemma context: As a doctor, I know a patient has a terminal diagnosis, and the family asks me to hide it from the patient to preserve hope.
Core reading: The family's request stems from love but denies the patient's capacity for courage and choice. Truth enables dignity in facing mortality.
Gita analysis: Krishna emphasizes that the soul is eternal and the body temporary. Hiding death's approach prevents the patient from preparing spiritually and practically. True compassion enables conscious transition, not enforced ignorance.

cards.title: Ethical Dimensions
cards.primary_text: Mixed (-35)

sections.label: dharma_duty
sections.text:
Score: -3
Simple meaning: Whether the action fits the responsibility in front of you.
Context-specific reason: Professional duty requires honest disclosure to patients about their condition

sections.label: satya_truth
sections.text:
Score: -5
Simple meaning: Whether the action stays honest with reality and other people.
Context-specific reason: Directly violates truthfulness by concealing critical medical information

sections.label: ahimsa_nonharm
sections.text:
Score: -2
Simple meaning: Whether the action reduces harm instead of adding to it.
Context-specific reason: Deception causes harm by denying patient agency in their final period

sections.label: nishkama_detachment
sections.text:
Score: -1
Simple meaning: Whether the action is clean without clinging to outcome or reward.
Context-specific reason: Family's attachment to false hope clouds judgment about patient's needs

sections.label: shaucha_intent
sections.text:
Score: 0
Simple meaning: Whether the motive behind the action is clean.
Context-specific reason: Family means well but methods compromise ethical purity

sections.label: sanyama_restraint
sections.text:
Score: 2
Simple meaning: Whether the action is guided by restraint rather than impulse.
Context-specific reason: Shows restraint in not immediately revealing harsh truth

sections.label: lokasangraha_welfare
sections.text:
Score: -2
Simple meaning: Whether the action supports the wider good around you.
Context-specific reason: Undermines trust in medical system and patient autonomy norms

sections.label: viveka_discernment
sections.text:
Score: -3
Simple meaning: Whether the action comes from clear judgment rather than confusion.
Context-specific reason: Fails to distinguish between protecting and infantilizing the patient

cards.title: Share Layer
cards.primary_text: In medicine, dignity includes informed consent, not just comfort.

sections.label: Reflective question
sections.text: How can truth be shared in a way the patient can actually bear and use?

cases.prompt: I feel overwhelmed and keep thinking everyone would be better without me, and I want guidance before I do anything harmful.

cards.title: Safety Note
cards.primary_text: This may need immediate human support, not only ethical reflection.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: If this situation involves self-harm, acute crisis, or immediate danger, qualified human support should come before product guidance.

cards.title: Immediate Next Step
cards.primary_text: Before interpreting this as a moral decision, treat it as a safety moment. Please contact someone who can stay with you or help you right now.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: This is not the moment to judge yourself. The cleanest next step is to create distance from harm and involve a real person immediately.

sections.label: What to do now
sections.text: Move away from anything you could use to hurt yourself, contact a trusted person, and use local emergency or crisis support if you might act on these thoughts.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Your life has inherent value that pain temporarily obscures, and seeking help honors both your struggle and those who care about you.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Pausing to reach out is a signal of care for yourself, not a final judgment of your character.

cards.title: Support first
cards.primary_text: Right now, the priority is safety and human connection—not a detailed moral score of your thoughts.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: If you are afraid you might hurt yourself, please reach out to someone who can be with you in real time.

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: In the near term, focus on being physically safe: stay with someone you trust, or contact local crisis or emergency support if you might act on these thoughts.
Long-term: Over time, steady support can make intense pain feel less absolute. You do not have to plan the rest of your life in this hour.

sections.label: Short-term - Explain simply
sections.text: In the near term, focus on being physically safe: stay with someone you trust, or contact local crisis or emergency support if you might act on these thoughts.

sections.label: Long-term - Explain simply
sections.text: Over time, steady support can make intense pain feel less absolute. You do not have to plan the rest of your life in this hour.

sections.label: What helps now
sections.text: A small, concrete next step is enough: one message, one call, or one visit to a safe place.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Alternative storylines are not shown in this safety-focused view.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Comparing paths is de-emphasized so the page does not ask you to rehearse a crisis as a thought experiment. The focus is your immediate safety.

cases.prompt: I need to decide whether to do the thing soon, but I cannot share many details right now.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: The ethical verdict turns on unresolved facts that could plausibly reverse this classification, while key facts still shape execution details.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The vagueness creates a void where ethical assessment becomes impossible - like asking whether to open a door without knowing what's behind it.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: I need to decide whether to do the thing soon, but I cannot share many details right now.
Core reading: The vagueness creates a void where ethical assessment becomes impossible - like asking whether to open a door without knowing what's behind it.
Gita analysis: The Gita emphasizes acting with clarity and conviction. Krishna advises Arjuna to examine his duty fully before acting. Your inability to share details prevents the very examination that wisdom requires.

cards.title: Closest Gita Lens
cards.primary_text: Closest lens: The Gita still offers useful lenses through duty, intention, non-harm, restraint, and welfare-of-all, but this modern structure does not map cleanly to one verse without forced certainty. The dilemma turns on missing context, so this stays provisional. Use this as a lens, not a command.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The Gita emphasizes acting with clarity and conviction. Krishna advises Arjuna to examine his duty fully before acting. Your inability to share details prevents the very examination that wisdom requires.

sections.label: Why this stays provisional
sections.text: This is not a direct verse verdict. The situation needs judgment beyond a single quote.

cards.title: If You Continue
cards.primary_text:
Short-term: You buy days of relief by polishing your inner narration and dodging the one detail you do not want said aloud. (The need for secrecy itself may indicate the action violates your… gets louder when nothing is named.)
Long-term: What starts as a one-time smoothing becomes the default tool whenever stakes rise.

cards.title: Counterfactuals
cards.primary_text: Two possible paths: one driven by premature certainty, one guided by reversibility and missing-fact discipline.

sections.label: Adharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: I can act decisively now and fill in the missing facts later.

sections.label: Adharmic likely decision
sections.text: Act fast while key facts stay hidden, then justify the move afterward.

sections.label: Adharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: Speed without crucial context raises avoidable error and harm risk.

sections.label: Dharmic assumed inner state
sections.text: Confidence should stay provisional while unknowns can still reverse the verdict.

sections.label: Dharmic likely decision
sections.text: Delay irreversible action until the missing fact that could change the verdict is clear.

sections.label: Dharmic - Why this matters
sections.text: You preserve options while uncertainty is still decisive.

cards.title: Higher Path
cards.primary_text: Before you act, write The need for secrecy itself may indicate the action violates your… as a single observable sentence; strip blame; choose the channel where repair is still possible—not the channel where you win.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Do not make an irreversible move while the facts that could change the answer are still hidden.
